segments.s010-g1: دل روشن من is a poetic, slightly inverted possessive phrase: دل (noun) + روشن (adjective) + من (possessive 'my'), literally 'my bright heart.' چو here = 'when.' برگشت is the simple past (3rd singular) of برگشتن 'to turn/return.' از اوی = 'from him' (اوی is an older/poetic form of او + enclitic), so the whole clause = 'when my bright heart turned away from him.'
segments.s010-g2: سوی 'toward' + تخت 'throne' + شاه 'king' + جهان 'world' = 'toward the throne of the king of the world.' کرد is the past auxiliary/lexical verb 'did/made' used with روی (face) to form روی کردن 'to turn one's face.' روی here = 'face,' so کرد روی = 'turned (its) face.' Word order is typical Persian verb-final in the clause (direction then object then verb).
segments.s010-g3: که introduces a subordinate/purpose clause 'so that/that.' این نامه را uses را as the definite object marker. دست پیش آوردن is an idiom 'to present/hold forward by hand' (دست + پیش + آوردن); آورم is 1st-person subjunctive/jussive ('that I may bring' / 'that I should bring'), so the clause = 'that I may present this letter in my hand.'
segments.s010-g4: ز (poetic form of از) 'from' + دفتر 'the record/register.' به گفتار خویش = 'into my own speech/words' (خویش is a reflexive/possessive form: 'one's own'); آورم here has the sense 'relate/report' — 'to bring/translate from the record into my own words.' The verb is again 1st-person subjunctive/jussive matching the preceding که-purpose clause.
segments.s010-g5: بپرسیدم is the past 1st-person form 'I asked' (the initial b- is a classical/poetic prefix seen in some older forms). از هر کسی = 'from every/each person.' بی‌شمار is an adverb/adjective 'countless/innumerable,' modifying the extent of asking. The clause = 'I asked innumerable people.'
segments.s010-g6: بترسیدم is past 1st-person 'I feared' or 'I was afraid.' از governs the cause/object of fear: از گردش روزگار = 'of the turning/course of time' (a poetic way to say 'fate' or 'the vicissitudes of life'). گردش here = 'turn/circuit' of روزگار 'time/fate.'
segments.s010-g7: مگر here functions as 'unless.' خود is emphatic/reflexive 'myself.' درنگم is 'my pause/hesitation' (درنگ + possessive -م). نباشد is a negative subjunctive/present form 'there be not' used in conditional sense after مگر ('unless there be/ unless I pause'). بسی is an intensifier 'much/indeed' (here adding force: 'indeed'/'very').
segments.s010-g8: بباید (باید with an initial ب-; here modal 'must/ought') expresses necessity. When followed by an infinitive noun like سپردن ('to entrust'), the construction means 'it must be entrusted' or 'one must entrust.' به دیگر کسی = 'to another person.' The overall sense: 'it must be entrusted to someone else.'
segments.s010-g17: اگر introduces a conditional clause (“if …”). این سخن is the noun phrase “this speech”; از + خدای marks source/agency (“from God”). In classical word order the conditional particle comes first and the rest of the clause follows without an overt copula.
segments.s010-g18: This is the apodosis/question following the if-clause. نبی is a vocative/title (“O Prophet” / “the Prophet”) addressed here; کی is an interrogative “who/then who”; نزد ما is a prepositional phrase “to/for us” or “in our presence”; and رهنمای is the noun ‘guide’ that the question asks about. Note the elliptical classical style: a copula or auxiliary is omitted and the pieces form a single rhetorical question.
segments.s010-g19: به شهرم is a locative expression “in/at my city” (classical texts often use به with place); یکی مهربان دوست بود is an indefinite noun phrase “there was a certain kind friend” with بود as the past copula ‘was’. Adjective (مهربان) follows the numeral/indefinite one (یکی) before the head noun (دوست).
segments.s010-g20: تو گفتی is “you said” (past); that clause is introduced by که (“that”) and contains با من “with me / toward me” plus the simile به یک پوست بود, literally “was to (me) as one skin” — an idiom meaning extremely close/attached. Note the object/order: classical Persian freely places pronouns and similes after the verb.
segments.s010-g21: مرا گفت is the inverted order “(he) said to me” (مرا = to me; here written as separate m + را in the manuscript form). آمد خوب (خوب آمد) is a fixed phrase meaning “it pleased (me)” or “it came well”; این رای تو = “this opinion/plan of yours.” The direct speech/quotation is implicit in the sequence.
segments.s010-g22: به نیکی گراید همی پای تو is a benediction: به نیکی ‘toward goodness’, گراید (subjunctive/optative sense ‘may … turn/ incline’), همی is an emphatic adverb ‘ever/indeed’, پای تو ‘your footing/fortune’ (پای used metaphorically). Together: “may your step/fortune turn to goodness.”
segments.s010-g23: 'نبشته' here functions as a noun/past-participle 'that which is written' (the written thing). The possessor 'من' follows it in poetic word order, so 'نبشته من' = 'my writing' or 'what I have written.' The following noun phrase 'این نامه پهلوی' specifies that written thing: 'this Pahlavi letter' (note adjective 'پهلوی' follows 'نامه').
segments.s010-g24: 'به پیش' = 'in the presence of / before'; 'تو' = 'you' (object). The verb 'آرم' is first-person present in a poetic/archaic form meaning 'I place / I lay (before you)' — (the literal dictionary senses given list a different meaning, so here it should be read as a poetic variant of 'آورم/بذارم'). 'مگر' introduces a negative purpose/circumstance 'lest / so that not', and 'نغنوی' is 'ن-' (negative) + present form of 'غنود' ('to sleep'), i.e. 'you do not fall asleep.' The clause therefore expresses purpose: 'I lay it before you so that you will not sleep.'
segments.s010-g9: و is the coordinating conjunction 'and' linking to the preceding remark. دیگر که introduces a new consideration ('another/otherwise, since/now that') and that-clause follows with the subject گنجم and predicate وفادار نیست; the verb نیست is a simple present/past-state negation ('is not') placed after the adjective as the copula, typical SOV order with the copula final.
segments.s010-g10: همین رنج را is a definite, fronted object phrase (همین 'this very' + رنج 'suffering' + the object marker را). کس خریدار نیست is a negated existential/predicative clause where خریدار (buyer/taker) is a predicate noun and کس ('no one') is the logical subject; نیست follows as the copula/negator, so literally 'this very suffering, no one is a buyer (of it)'.
segments.s010-g11: بر این‌گونه is a prepositional/manner phrase ('in this way/thus') placed before the duration phrase یک چند ('a little while'); بگذاشتم is first-person past tense 'I left/put off' (verb at the end), so the whole means 'I left it so for a while.'
segments.s010-g12: سخن را is the definite direct object ('the saying/poem/speech' + object marker را). نهفته is an adjective/participial complement meaning 'hidden,' and همی داشتم is an archaic/perfect-imperfect construction: همی is an old continuous/aspective particle and داشتم is past-tense 'I had/kept,' together 'I kept (it) hidden.' The order is object — adjective complement — auxiliary verb.
segments.s010-g13: سراسر زمانه is a noun phrase 'throughout the age' or 'the whole age' (سراسر modifying زمانه). پر از جنگ بود is the predicate 'was full of war' with پر 'full' taking از+noun to indicate contents; بود is the past copula 'was.'
segments.s010-g14: به جویندگان is a dative/experiencer phrase 'for the seekers' or 'to seekers' placed frontally for emphasis. بر جهان تنگ بود is a predication 'the world was narrow' (تنگ 'narrow/constrained') with بر indicating the domain ('upon the world'); بود is the copula in past tense, so literally 'for seekers, the world was narrow.'
segments.s010-g15: ز نیکو سخن is a prepositional/genitive phrase 'of/for good speech' (ز = 'of/from' in poetic register). به چه اندر جهان is a rhetorical question 'to what [purpose/extent] in the world' (به چه = 'for what/what worth'), with اندر 'in' and جهان 'the world'; word order is inverted for poetic effect.
segments.s010-g16: به نزد is a locative/prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before.' سخن‌سنج is a compound noun 'speech-critic' (head noun), and فرخ مهان appears as a name or epithet following it (modifier position), so the whole means 'before the speech-critic Farrokh Mahan.'
segments.s010-g25: This is a nominal clause with two coordinated predicates followed by the copula هست. گشاده‌زبان is an adjectival compound meaning “outspoken” and stands first; و links it to جوانیت. جوانیت is جوان + the second-person possessive/nominalizer -ت, here meaning “your youth.” هست is the copula ‘is/are’ placed after the predicates (typical in classical/poetic Persian).
segments.s010-g26: سخن گفتن is a compound verbal noun/infinitive (سخن + گفتن) meaning “to speak / speaking (words)” and functions as the subject. پهلوانیت is an abstract/predicative noun built on پهلوان meaning (the quality or state of a) ‘hero/champion’ — here ‘your heroism’ or ‘heroic quality.’ هست is the copula again, so the whole clause reads ‘To speak is your heroism.’
segments.s010-g27: شو is the imperative of شدن (an archaic/poetic use meaning roughly “go/act” or “be so” as a command). این نامه is the direct object “this letter.” خسروان (kings) is the indirect/dative target — typical poetic word order can place object before its addressee without an explicit preposition. باز + گوی here are imperatives/adverb + verb: باز (‘again’/‘openly’) modifies گوی, and گوی is an archaic or poetic imperative form of گفتن (‘say’/‘tell’).
segments.s010-g28: بدین جوی is a prepositional/adverbial phrase meaning “in this manner” (بدین = به + این ‘by/with this’, جوی = ‘manner/way’ in poetic usage). نزد مهان is another prepositional phrase ‘in the presence of / before the guests’ (نزد = ‘before, in the presence of’, مهان = ‘guests’). آبروی is the nominal predicate ‘honor/reputation’ with the copula omitted (a common nominal sentence in Persian poetry); the sense is ‘thus the guests will have honor.’
segments.s010-g29: چو is a conjunction here meaning ‘when.’ آورد is the past (3rd sing.) of آوردن ‘to bring.’ این نامه is the object ‘this letter.’ نزدیک من is an adverbial phrase ‘near me / to me’ (نزدیک = ‘near’, من = ‘me’). Word order places the verb before its object, a common poetic/older Persian option.
segments.s010-g30: بر افروخت is a past transitive compound verb (بر‑افروخت) meaning ‘kindled / set alight / lit up’; بر is a separable/prepositional prefix here giving a sense of ‘upon/over.’ این جان تاریک من is the object phrase ‘this my dark soul’ with این as demonstrative, جان ‘soul/heart,’ تاریک ‘dark’ modifying جان, and من postposed to mark possession (‘my’), which is an older Persian pattern (noun + pronoun = possessive).

sentences.s010-s1: When my bright heart turned away from him, it turned its face toward the throne of the king of the world.
sentences.s010-s2: that I might put this letter forward in my hand, and bring it from the register into my own words.
sentences.s010-s3: I asked countless people; I feared the turning of fate.
sentences.s010-s4: Unless I myself paused, it would still be necessary to entrust (it) to another person.
sentences.s010-s9: If this speech is not from God, O Prophet, who then will be our guide?
sentences.s010-s10: In my city there was a certain kind friend; you said he was to me like one skin.
sentences.s010-s11: He told me, ‘This counsel of yours pleases me well; may your fortunes turn to goodness.’
sentences.s010-s12: I have written this Pahlavi letter and lay it before you so that you do not fall asleep.
sentences.s010-s5: And furthermore, since my gancam/ganzam (my treasure) is not faithful, there is no one to accept this very hardship.
sentences.s010-s6: So I left things like that for a little while and kept the matter hidden.
sentences.s010-s7: The whole age was full of war; for seekers the world was narrow.
sentences.s010-s8: What place is there for fine speech in the world, in the presence of the critic Farrokh Mahan?
sentences.s010-s13: You are outspoken and young; speaking is your heroism.
sentences.s010-s14: Go and present this letter to the kings; speak it openly — in this way the guests will gain honor.
sentences.s010-s15: When this letter was brought near me, it kindled my dark soul.
